item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*clicks collapsible at the end* ರ_ರ

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1. Anyone else wondering what happened to that family?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Those logs are just fantastic. My favorite flavor of weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is absolutely marvelous. And it still would have been if it was just "a bunch of horses that make you forget what horses are", but there's so much more to it, and you don't come right out and say anything. :D Well, aside from that final picture. But apparently, it was necessary for some! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I read "compressed toenails", I thought to myself, "horse? nah, can't be horse".
And yet, it could.
+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had a completely different image of the SCP in my head before I saw the final image.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can someone explain what's going on here. I have read this couple of times but I still don't quite get what's going on except that something is going on with horses.

Really? +100? Well, alright.

I guess you guys have asked for it.

The Cowboy (and possibly his Steeds) will appear again, it is now my sworn duty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Considering horses have to be maintained, fed, and cared for, you can't exactly lock them in a box and be done with it. Euclid seems more than reasonable in this case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the SCP but I definitely think the object class should be “safe”. Horses are not difficult to contain at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I managed to figure it out at "compressed toenails", but read through the rest anyway.

Genuinely creepy even after realizing what they really were. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There was something that I really liked about this article that I couldn't quite place, and then it hit me:
The structure of this article means that the Foundation doesn't know what horses are.
If they did, they'd say the Steeds are physically identical to non-anomalous horses. If there's some rule against saying that in the same way that calling them horses seems to be a bad idea, they'd say that. The format of this article means that either the Steeds (or something else) have some property that has totally blotted out the concept of "horse", or all horses, everywhere, are anomalous. Which is honestly even creepier.

+1 for horses what they shouldn't be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is impressively unnerving, and I think the end actually makes it creepier.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm in the same park as Baronjoe. The unsettling image my mind came up with added to the creep factor of the DVD. As I read I was really hoping the "steeds" wouldn't turn out to be just another "(animal) but we can't recognize them as (animal)," which I feel like has been done many times already. The connection to Doublehorses at the end doesn't do anything for me since I don't like that tale. My upvote comes from the DVD transcripts because I really like their eeriness, and its been awhile since an article has genuinely unsettled me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Other than possibly being related to Doublehorses, I wrote this with no other site lore connection in mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could this be related to the end-of-knowledge events from one of the doomsday entries? I can't remember which one it is, but it involved humans forgetting about pets.

item.title: This is art
item.content:encoded:
One of my favourite things in SCPs is when something exactly rides the line between being inexplicable and having an underlying associative logic, and this is frankly a masterpiece at just that.

Gonna do a spoiler because this might spoil the magic for some folks:

Analytical spoilers
– hide block

The idea of the article is that the steeds are almost but not quite horses, in an uncanny way that never quite resolves in your mind (hence the headaches it gives you, and all the more so the more you know about horses).

So how do you sell the idea of something that's almost but not quite horse? Several ways, some more obvious than others. Obviously we scrupulously avoid mention of horses directly; we call them "steeds", a word that almost means horses exclusively but not quite; we deescribe them only in terms of anatomy from other animals or at too close a focus for the mental picture to resolve to "horse".

But the real engine making this work is associative logic: the article takes concepts that are themselves almost but not quite related to horses, and has them orbit the "steeds" in ways that also don't quite make immediate sense:

  • Salt is the most used concept of this kind, and it returns throughout the article. Salt is an expertly chosen material for this purpose, because it has a weak but present associative link to horses (because people give salt licks to their horses), and it's a pleasingly mundane material (this fitting with the overall agricultural milieu) but it's also rich with other associations (e.g. its used often in many occult traditions in rituals involving the binding of banishment of demons - which is why we see the cowboy laying a circle of it). Salt is persistently referred to so we recognise it as a non-arbitrary detail,but also persistently refuses to be associated with horses in an explicable and expected manner, so encouraging us to dwell on the other associations.
  • The trowel in the second part of the video also operates this way; it's dimly associated with horses as a recognisably agricultural implement, but its actual use on a horse-like creature is not immediately clear - what can you dig in a horse? and we cut away before any further detail is seen).
  • The cattle prod in the final bit of video works the same way: cattle prods are almost but not quite something you use on horses, and we cut away before seeing how its used. But also note how its appearance at that juncture in "How Steed Were Made" represents a resurgence of the agricultural logic in what was otherwise a very occult scene of magical body parts coalescing.

I could honestly write a whole essay. I think I might. I wish I could +1 twice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"How Steed Were Made […] After a minute, several individual pieces of SCP-4466 instances float upwards and begin to form a loose ball above the pit. PoI-4466 […] pulls out a cattle prod"

The anomaly definitely seems like more than just affecting how people perceive them.

As for my opinion though, I'll just say I was afraid to open the image without checking the discussion first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Noted~!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will say, someone had this same theory before, and, while you can believe any headcanon, this was not my intention. Even if you didn’t see “The Dangers of Misunderstanding Your Steed”, SCP-4466 would still be anomalous

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dear Christ. Instant classic.

ETA: I normally don't say this, but I'd love some spoilers eventually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh my god, I felt like such an idiot when opening that final image. Well played.

…wait. Is the real anomaly a memetic influence from the DVD that makes people think horses steeds are anomalous creatures?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hot holy christ!

This entire thing made me incredibly uncomfortable. I have no idea what it is with the SCP and horses, but the combination leads to some very dark and strange places.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Genuinely disquieting. Congratulations. I was reminded of "In The Barn" by Piers Anthony.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad to be of service <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I read the description, I was disturbed by what my mind came up with. As things progressed and finally got to the reveal, I just went "oh."

Clever but kinda feels like a -J due to how it is presented. Nonetheless, I upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's Wilson's Wildlife Solutions?

But in all seriousness, I think this works better without GoI ties.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read this in chat, and I still want to slightly strangle you for making me so anxious while reading it.

Spoiler
– hide block

I still don't know how you made reading about goddamn normal horses uncomfortable.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this should be part of Wilson's Wildlife Solutions

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Picture attributions: Horse and barn.

Thank you: Ihp, multizig, Meserach

Usually I do the picture sources in the article so I don't have to waste space on the author post for it, but I wanted the crosslink at the end of the article to be where it was. So. The pictures are currently not locally sourced but they will be later.

Spoilers
Oh No

These aren't doublehorses. But you can assume our cowboy knows of the house of inconstant horses, probably by a different name if I were to guess… Otherwise, I ain't spilling my hand.

Are they horses, though? If they were horses, wouldn't we call them horses? It's not like this exists in a world without horses.

EDIT: this feels relevant

EDIT2: I made some art!
I think this is a bit over-the-top as compared to what actually makes the skip scary, but ah well, I had fun making it.
